data.title: Northern Dynasty Minerals Seeks A Summary Judgment After EPA Settlement Talks Stall
data.description:
Northern Dynasty Minerals (NYSE:NAK) was dealt a blow with the inability to reach a settlement with the EPA around withdrawing its veto. Northern Dynasty has now filed a motion in the Federal District Court in Alaska seeking a summary judgment in its litigation over the veto. While there is still a chance that the veto will be overturned in the courts, it would have been significantly better for Northern Dynasty if it was able to reach a settlement.

That being said, I have moved to a near-term neutral outlook on the stock based on trading value. It is currently pricing in a roughly 20% to 25% chance of the veto being removed through court actions, which seems generally reasonable to me.

I am still more skeptical about Pebble in the longer-term since there are still financing issues and continued legal battles even if Northern Dynasty can achieve a near-term court victory.

This report uses US dollars as the base currency, along with an exchange rate of CAD$1.37 to US$1.00.

EPA's Stance

Northern Dynasty's July 4 announcement that it was negotiating with the EPA to explore a potential settlement helped push its stock up by over 60% by July 16.

I viewed this announcement as being a modest positive, but based on the wording, didn't consider it to be a done deal. Words such as "negotiating to explore a potential settlement" left a reasonable amount of doubt that the veto would be voluntarily withdrawn.

In the end, after months of abeyances, Northern Dynasty and the EPA were unable to reach a settlement and it appears that the EPA will attempt to defend the veto in the courts.

Court Case

Northern Dynasty is now seeking a summary judgment in its litigation over the veto, attempting to move matters ahead more quickly.

The court case is being overseen by Judge Sharon Gleason. Judge Gleason was appointed by President Obama and has ruled both for and against environmental groups before.

With the EPA apparently defending the veto, I am assuming 20% odds of success for Northern Dynasty. Judge Gleason is seen as fair, but she seems to be politically at least somewhat to the left of President Trump's EPA.

The case can be appealed to the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. Historically, 81% of Judge Gleason's decisions are upheld on appeal.

Insider Transactions

Many Northern Dynasty insiders reported selling shares during this last week, with so far over 3 million shares reported sold at roughly around the mid-$2 to $3 range (in CAD).

Some of the sales were connected to recently exercised options, as Northern Dynasty had approximately 6.7 million options (with a CAD$2.01 exercise price) expiring on July 17, 2025. The 6.7 million options include those owned by non-insiders who would not need to report their transactions.

This insider selling was not that surprising given that I believed Northern Dynasty's near-term upside was a bit over US$2 per share if there was a veto removal. Northern Dynasty's share price closed at US$2.30 on July 16, which basically fully priced in a veto removal despite there still being uncertainty about what would happen.

Notes On Valuation

In terms of near-term prices, I continue to believe that a veto removal would result in a US$2+ share price for Northern Dynasty, while a full court loss could drop its share to around US$0.60 per share.

A 20% chance of success in the courts would thus result in a expected value in the US$0.90s per share.

Northern Dynasty should have enough cash to last into 2027 currently, after the receipt of the third US$12 million tranche payment earlier this year, and the proceeds from exercised options.

Thus I am moving to a near-term neutral outlook on Northern Dynasty as a trading stock, although I am still more skeptical about its longer-term value.

This is a very lengthy process, as I had noted back in 2020 when I gave Pebble a 20% chance of getting into production by the end of 2029. Even with a near-term court victory, Pebble won't be able get into production during this decade now.

Conclusion

Northern Dynasty's share price took a big hit after it was unable to come to a settlement agreement with the EPA. Its share price was essentially pricing in a 100% chance of success, but now I would estimate its odds at around 20% around succeeding through near-term court actions.

I am now neutral on Northern Dynasty at around US$1 per share based on my estimated odds of court success.

Longer-term I am more skeptical of Northern Dynasty's value since it still faces other hurdles in making Pebble into a producing mine. I think there is a significant chance that the project is in a similar spot several years from now.

data.title: DIVZ: Active Dividend ETF With Low-Risk Profile
data.description:
DIVZ strategy

Opal Dividend Income ETF (NYSEARCA:DIVZ) is an actively managed ETF launched on 1/27/21 with the objective “to provide capital appreciation with lower volatility and a higher dividend yield compared to the S&P 500 Index”. It has a portfolio of 30 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 3.19%, and an expense ratio of 0.65%. Distributions are paid monthly.

As described in the prospectus by TrueShares, the fund

…seeks to achieve its investment objective by purchasing 25-35 stocks of companies that pay dividends and expect to grow the dividends over time and are trading at attractive valuations.

Selection criteria include: above-average dividend yields, ability and commitment to sustain and grow dividends; management team quality, compelling free cash flow growth and valuation ratios.

The portfolio turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 80%. This article will use as a benchmark the S&P 500 Index, represented by SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

Portfolio

The fund is mostly invested in U.S. companies (89% of asset value), with a focus on large and megacaps (69%). The heaviest sector in the portfolio is consumer staples, with 26% of asset value, followed by energy (13.9%). Compared to the S&P 500, DIVZ massively overweights consumer staples, energy, utilities, and materials. It greatly downplays technology, consumer discretionary, industrials, and ignores real estate.

The portfolio is quite concentrated. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 48.7% of asset value, and the top names weigh about 6%.

Performance

DIVZ has underperformed SPY by 1.8 percentage points in annualized return since 2/3/2021, with a lower risk measured by historical volatility and maximum drawdown. The risk-adjusted performance stays inferior to the benchmark, though (Sharpe ratio in the table below).

The annual sum of distributions has decreased by 24.3% in three years, from $1.11 per share in 2021 to $0.84 in 2024. In the same time, the cumulative inflation was about 13% (based on CPI), resulting for shareholders in a loss of 34% in inflation-adjusted income.

Competitors

DIVZ has the highest expense ratio and very weak trading volumes compared to these funds. On the upside, it is among the least volatile and comes in second position behind VYM for total return since February 2021.

Takeaway

Opal Dividend Income ETF has a concentrated portfolio of 30 dividend stocks selected based on quality and valuation. DIVZ has a focus on consumer staples and strong value characteristics. The track record of DIVZ is too short to assess its long-term potential, and it is mixed for now. Distribution history shows a concerning downtrend, yet the fund reaches its objective of cutting volatility while keeping a decent total return. DIVZ fares well compared to the most popular dividend ETFs, although VYM displays better results with a lower expense ratio and much higher trading volumes. The main drawbacks of DIVZ are fees, low liquidity, and short history.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Flipping Burgers - McDonald's And Restaurant Brands International Revisited
data.description:
McDonald’s (NYSE:MCD) is a company that needs no introduction due to a brand that is loud and proudly represented worldwide. Restaurant Brands International (NYSE:QSR) is less well known as a stock, but flagship brands like Burger King and Tim Hortons are easily recognisable.

While each company has its nuances, and pro’s and con’s, I see them as highly comparable competitors that can be traded as a pair. I covered this thesis in an article in April this year in which I compared the two firms in terms of fundamentals, but more importantly valuation.

The summary was that both are high-quality companies, which can fit well in a long term diversified portfolio as ‘buy and hold’ stocks. However, as the valuations between the two diverge, my preferred strategy is to switch positions when the valuations dictate. MCD is a great long term value creator, and has rewarded shareholders well over the long term. QSR has been much more volatile, has higher leverage, but offers faster growth. QSR share price fluctuations over time have created regular buying opportunities.

I outlined a simple “Flipping Burger” strategy, to capitalise on the valuation deviation that I have observed. Check out the article in the link above for the full story.

A quarter on, I am checking in on the pair.

Price Performance

So far the thesis on valuation reversion has played out. QSR has appreciated by 9.4%, while MCD has declined by 5.2%, nearly a 15% outperformance by QSR.

Valuation

Price to earnings TTM shows the picture well. With the high debt load, broader product base, and lower global brand recognition QSR should trade at a discount to MCD. A 10% P/E multiple discount seems like an appropriate gap. On this metric, the pair seems to be fairly valued on a comparative basis.

However on a forward P/E basis, QSR still appears to offer better value, due to the more positive earnings outlook than MCD.

What’s behind this is the analyst forward earnings estimates.

For QSR, the year on year earnings growth for the upcoming quarter is over 12%. 2 year forward earnings estimates for 2025 and 2026 are for 10.14% growth, compared to a more modest 6.4% earnings growth estimated for MCD. The table below is from Seeking Alpha data.

The main drivers for this at QSR are the progress in the turnaround of the Burger King brand under the 'Reclaim the Fire' initiative, continued growth of the international segment, and a continued tailwind at home in Canada with the 'Canada First' shift in consumer preferences to support local brands in the light of the ongoing U.S. trade tensions.

Turning to price to cash flow, the valuation range is tighter, with MCD coming out ahead by a margin of around 5%. This reflects a stronger cash flow yield being generated by MCD.

QSR FCF has fluctuated with the more volatile earnings over the years. While we don't have forward FCF guidance, the improving overall sales and margins should also reflect in a continuation of the FCF improvement that can be seen over the last reporting year.

Conversely, MCD saw a slight deterioration in FCF over the 2023/2024 period. This is likely the result of higher capex invested via the 'Accelerate The Arches' initiative. Higher capex can be expected through 2025 due to the continued investment, resulting in worsening FCF, with management banking on sales growth the follow on from the capex investment in 2026, and improve FCF.

In a nutshell I'd expect to see QSR FCF improve on a forward basis, and MCD FCF decline, resulting in a wider discount in QSR valuation in terms of price to forward FCF than is shown above.

QSR has a stronger franchise vs owned store mic than MCD, which enables more of the investment costs in funding sales and store improvement to be passed through to franchisees. For MCD, sales growth conversion to FCF is likely to be less efficient than QSR.

The Analyst View

Overall, both SA Analysts and SA Quant rate MCD as a hold, with poor scores for valuation and growth, offset by profitability and positive earnings revisions.

Wall St analysts have MCD as a Buy.

Wall St average price target is for a 10.7% upside. Note the large deviation between the high and the low price target.

QSR enjoys a much more positive analyst sentiment, with Buy ratings from all three sources.

SA quant scores QSR higher across most categories. Valuation is only rated a C-, which is one to watch.

Wall St has only a slightly higher price target for QSR than for MCD, with an 11.2% average price target. The high vs low deviation is also pronounced.

Conclusion

So far the strategy to flip from MCD to QSR has worked out well, generating 15% alpha over the last quarter. As one has drifted up, and the other drifted down, the valuation gap has narrowed, but is still pronounced on both TTM and forward earnings.

While FCF the valuation for MCD is now below that of QSR on a TTM basis, I expect to see the FCF for MCD to reduce, and for QSR to accelerate over the remainder of 2025.

As such, I don't think that it is time to flip the pair just yet. I will be watching the Q2 earnings of each for updates on the valuation metrics and outlook. If I see the MCD premium erode to just 10% across the metric, this will be a trigger to flip from QSR to MCD.

data.title: Leonardo DRS: Impressive Company That's Highly Valued
data.description:
Introduction

Leonardo DRS, Inc. (NASDAQ:DRS) is a defense technology company based in Arlington, Virginia. The company operates in Advanced Sensing and Computing and Integrated Mission Systems segments. The first segment focuses on building a suite of products that can be called "real time situational awareness" solutions which allow the military to collect data from many different platforms and sources and make effective decisions quickly. The second segment actually utilizes a lot of technology developed by the first segment in order to offer a integrated package of defense solutions that utilize both land and naval resources

Business Overview

The company designs, develops, and manufactures integrated solutions for the military, intelligence services, and other military contractors. The company was originally founded in the 1960s during the Cold War, and its main products mainly focused on detection and disruption of enemy submarines which was a vital task at the time. Now the company is using its many years of experience in defensive technology development to create more modern and integrative solutions.

The company uses a "platform-agnostic" approach to its product development which means that its products will work with any platform. The military works with many different contractors which creates a large number of platforms to deal with. Often times contractors' solutions will only work on their own platforms or a limited number of platforms. Leonardo focuses on a more flexible approach where products are designed in a way that allows them to work on multiple different platforms and integrate with other solutions. This is achieved both on the software and hardware side, giving the company a nice moat. Since the company doesn't build a platform of its own, it can focus its efforts on the entire defense ecosystem. This also makes it difficult to replace this company's products, giving it stickiness.

Strategic Focus

The company's strategic focus is wide, flexible, and adaptable but at the same time targeted and focused. How can these two things be true at the same time? Let me explain. The company's strategic focus shifts along with the military's own prioritization. The company periodically looks at what product types are in high demand in a given environment and where most growth opportunities can come from. When picking its strategic target, the company is very flexible and open to going in any direction, but once the target is picked and narrowed, the company shifts its focus entirely there. The company is not shy about canceling products that aren't delivering value in order to focus more on projects that create more value, but it is also open to shifting its priorities if the military's own priorities change. After all, the military is the biggest customer, and that's where the money comes from. If you insist on building products that your customer doesn't want, you will end up having no revenues no matter how great your products might be.

One example of this strategic shift is what the company has been doing for the last few years. It shifted its priorities into four areas: 1) autonomous, 2) dynamic, 3) interconnected, and 4) multi-domain capabilities. These also coincide with the military's own modernization priorities (for example see this document from Joint All-Domain Command and Control (JADC2)), and even though I used the word "coincide" this is no coincidence. The company's leadership actively tries to get feedback from the military through its well-established relationships to inform its strategic decisions. Besides, it's not like the military's new strategic shift towards more interconnectedness and more automation is a secret either.

Ownership Structure

As if this wasn't complicated enough, the company also has a unique ownership structure. At the same time it is both a subsidiary and a separate company. About 80% of the company's shares are owned by an Italian defense company named Leonardo S.p.a. (OTCPK:FINMF). The remaining 20% of the company is publicly traded, thus the ticker of DRS. This means the total number of shares floating in the market is actually only 1/5th of the company's total shares outstanding since Leonardo S.p.A. has no intention of selling any shares for the foreseeable future. This creates a scarcity of shares in the market considering there are very few shares available to buy. This probably helps the company's shares to carry a premium, but I wouldn't say the premium is as big as 1 to 5. This also doesn't mean that the Italian parent company gets to keep 80% of this company's profits. This means it gets to keep 80% of the dividends though since they own 80% of the shares and each share receives a dividend when the company chooses to pay it.

Product Pipeline

The company's pipeline includes many products with fancy futuristic names such as The Battlefield AI Processor (the AIP). The Battlefield AI Processor is similar to the self-driving feature we see in Tesla (TSLA) cars today, but in addition to driving it also includes battle management. It integrates with military vehicles like tanks or drones and collects and processes massive amounts of data that it collects from different tools such as cameras, thermal sensors, microphones, and radars at such a speed much faster than a human possibly could. Then it plans a course of action very quickly to determine what should be done next in line with the new data. Mind you, the data is not just collected and analyzed at one specific time. The data actually constantly flows in a stream, and the tool is constantly analyzing new data as it flows in, just the same way a self-driving vehicle would. It can also identify friendly forces, civilians, and enemies so that accidental casualties can be avoided.

There are also the new CMOSS/SOSA standards and regulations which dictate that electronic tools and components must now share the same connectors, the same power standards, and common software protocols. For example, all radios and computers should have interchangeable charging adapters as much as possible. These new regulations may benefit companies like Leonardo that utilize a platform-agnostic approach as I described above.

Financials

Now let us look at some of the company's metrics. In the last couple years the company's revenues exploded from about $2.5 billion to $3.5 billion. Similarly, the company's margin categories showed significant gains as its gross margin rose from 17% to 23%, operating margin rose from 7.5% to 9.2%, and EBIT margin rose from 8% to 9%. As a result of these, the company's net income rose at a rate higher than 150% since 2022.

The company's growth rate is significantly higher than its peers. The company's revenue growth is more than triple the growth rate of its sector, while its EBITDA growth rate is 200% higher than the sector average, and its EPS growth rate of 25% represents a 400% premium over its peers. Although the company's revenue growth rate slowed down from its 5-year average of 31%, it's still pretty significant at the current rate.

Valuation

However, much of this growth seems to be reflected in the company's valuation. The company trades at a significant premium against its peers as well as its own 5-year average with a current trailing P/E of 48 on a non-GAAP basis and 55 on a GAAP basis, significantly above the sector median of 20 to 24 and the company's own 5-year average of 33. Even if you focus entirely on the PEG metric which accounts for a company's growth rate, we are still getting a 68% premium against peers as the company's current PEG of 2.11 is compared to the sector median of 1.25. A fair value for this company would be a PEG of ~1.5 which would create a balance between its growth and value.

Conclusion

On one hand the company has a bright future with a successful suite of products, strong growth, and a significant technological moat but on the other hand its valuation is off the charts. It appears that investors are not blind to this company's greatness, and they have already been bidding it up for the last year or so. This seems to be the case with a lot of companies these days. Investors and traders bid up most companies to such valuations that there is very little room for error and the margin of safety is very thin. This also makes it very difficult for new investors to find value plays which creates a loop. If potential investors can't find any good value plays and still want to be invested, they start ignoring valuations. This, in return, makes it more and more difficult to find value plays, and more and more investors are lured into ignoring valuations because they observe that they don't seem to matter as past investors before they made a lot of money by ignoring valuations, but how sustainable is this really? If you already own this stock I would keep holding but if you haven't already bought, I'd wait for more reasonable valuations.

data.title: Apple's AI Pivot Changes Everything (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Until now, investors had viewed Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) as a haven of stable cash flow, crafted from secure earnings and incremental, risk-free innovation. But in today's rapidly evolving world of high tech, that old narrative is no longer whole. It is time to look more carefully at Apple as doing more than holding its profitability machine, but subtly redefining its DNA to stay ahead of the curve for the artificial-intelligence era.

Macroeconomic headwinds, record-high quarterly earnings per share, and habitual product cycle upgrades notwithstanding, Apple’s slow-growth track record, high relative valuation multiples to its peers, and tariff exposures all combine to create an asymmetric investment narrative that’s less black-and-white than the “safe haven” tale would suggest.

With revenue in Q2 of FY25 of $95.4 billion, an increment of 5% from the previous year, and net earnings rising 4.8% to $24.8 billion, Apple is showing its operational prowess. But beneath these beats to headlines are cracks in its structural moat, cracks that its AI narrative, transformed supply chain, and activist buyback policy work to fill before these become larger valuation risks.

Apple’s Q3: Solid Numbers Are Likely, But the Real Signal Lies in the Subtext

Consensus is forecasting revenue of $88.8 billion and EPS of $1.42, just 1.78% year-over-year growth. Business as usual, on paper, the services revenue keeps growing, hardware is steady, and Apple, as ever, by a thread, exceeds expectations.

However, below that glossy surface, there are more subtle developments worth consideration. The strength of mainland China, supported by price actions and region-specific promos, could stealthily offset soft hardware globally. While services, that is, subscription and App Store growth, fatten margins, making the EPS line appear sturdier than initially assumed.

Here’s where things get even more interesting: the market may already have the steady execution of Apple built into its calculations, so upside surprises would have less to do with numbers and more to do with narrative. Investors would have less to do with Apple attaining $1.42 in EPS and more to do with what is being stated regarding rollout of AI. If initial traction for Apple Intelligence shows actual user adoption, the opposite of buzz, the Street could begin to modify its long-range valuation model.

Threatened tariffs for key supply chains, and an unusually high number of downward estimate cuts, however, indicate risk. If Apple blunts forward guidance or waves caution due to trade policy or AI ramp-up cost, the stock could mark time even on the headline beat. In short, Q2 might look predictable on the surface, but the subtext could stealthily redefine Apple’s near-term narrative.

Innovation or Insulation? Looking More Closely at the Main Model

Apple’s business model continues to revolve around an unprecedented hardware moat that’s topped off with a high-margin Services layer. The latest results have Products revenue coming in at $68.7 billion, expanding a modest 3% YoY, while Services grew 12% to an all-time high of $26.6 billion, in evidence of management’s attempts to look past hardware cycles.

Notably enough, the iPhone still accounts for nearly half of all sales, rising 2% to $46.8 billion. Behind this steady headline, however, iPhone upgrade volumes grew in double digits, while unit ASPs showed signs of promotional activity and tariff protection, a soft reminder that stickiness in this ecosystem now depends increasingly on value-for-money perception.

This balancing act is clearly visible in Apple’s shipment dynamics. As of Q1 2025, iPhone’s global market share slipped to 19%, down from a holiday-driven 23% peak just a quarter prior. While Q2 2025 results show revenue resilience, the trailing shipment trend hints at a tougher backdrop for unit economics and global demand momentum.

Segments of Mac and iPad had spectacular recoveries, as revenue of Mac rose 7% to $7.9 billion and that of iPad rose 15% to $6.4 billion, led by chips of M-series and boosts to performance. Segment of Wearables, Home, and Accessories declined 5% to $7.5 billion due to cycles of discretionary spending and declining novelty of Watch Series and AirPods. The out-of-whack segment profile makes sense for Apple to double down on the flywheel of Services: gross margin of Services still looks terrific, 75.7%, but for Products, for the quarter, 35.9%.

The subtle genius, and risk, of Apple’s approach is its incremental slide into an annuity-like basis of subs. Over 1 billion reported paid subs now, repeat revenue is evening out cyclic hardware blips. Apple, however, is so tied to App Store policy, never mind serial antitrust lawsuits and Europe’s Digital Markets Act that looks set to put that high-margin stream to the test, that the latest earnings call described alternative stores for app subs as endemic to today’s results but 2025-2026’s wild card.

Competitive Moats Under Pressure: Tariffs, Supply Chain Renegotiation, and an Arms Race in AI

Apple's weakest link which is the global supply chain, has become a double-edged tool. Q2 proof of demand verification verified an overweight percentage of iPhones used in the U.S. now emanating out of India, as Vietnam is responsible for virtually all Macs, iPads, and AirPods for the United States market.

Such diversification attempts to compensate for an anticipated tariff drag of $900 million in Q3 FY25 should tensions on world commerce persist. The kicker is Apple’s CFO famously announcing that build-head inventory plans have blunted short-term tariff impact but won’t insulate it for all eternity. Given the Section 232 chip probe still being conducted, potential downstream chip tariffs remain a threat that would force Apple either to absorb higher costs or pass them along to the consumer, crowding segment margins already compressed by ASP.

Meanwhile, the AI narrative is being positioned to arrive preceded by an exit from hardware. Tim Cook’s emphasis on Apple Intelligence and in-device LLMs is fascinating; the motivation to locally process large-bag LLMs within the A18 chips and the M-series Macs give away the hand to the future where Apple creates its user privacy moat and remains an insider to the generative AI explosion.

Apple has an issue here as even as its R&D spend is rising (up 8% year-to-date year to $8.55 billion for the quarter), its capital outlays on base models and on data centers have not yet achieved hyperscale competition. This model of in-house model + selective (ChatGPT) integration makes sense, but still not transformational enough to command valuation premia like that of dedicated AI platform plays.

Competition for high-end users also remains. Apple contained its Greater China sales decline to 2%, or flat FX-neutral, as a result of Chinese subsidies, but the region remains susceptible to home-bred competition as well as nationalist sentiment. Flat 1.73%–6.44% forecasted YoY EPS range for quarters ahead refers to that challenge. Even as it achieves high-margin expansion of business lines for its Services segment, hardware’s marginal advantages ever more struggle to uphold.

Financial Strength and Valuation Expansion: Is There Room for Multiple Expansion?

Apple's balance sheet shows total liabilities of $264.4 billion against shareholder equity of $66.8 billion, illustrating its net cash position that still underlies buybacks but also shows the magnitude of leverage engaged. Future buyback authorization of an additional $100 billion seeks to mitigate dilution and seasonal EPS expansion but is by itself not an expansionary impetus.

Valuation these days is pretty much the problem. With ~30x P/E for FY25 and a forward 2026 P/E of ~26.7x, Apple’s sector premium makes it practically near-term overvalued. Its forward 2.7x PEG ratio (non-GAAP FWD) is 46% above the sector median, so that’s too great of a price paid for earnings growth that’s going to remain in the low-to-mid single digits. Even by EV/Sales at 7.7x, that blows past its sector’s 3.5x, a high 124% premium that is going to have to get repriced rightly through continued leadership on the margin front and exemplary buyback execution.

Bottom Line for Investors

Apple continues to be an operational excellence and brand longevity masterclass, yet its high-valuation premium now depends so much on a capital return engine and a yet unproven AI bet. Investors should view this as a high-grade compounder, but one for which its multiple now reacts to even a slight decline in growth or geopolitical weakness. Long-term holders might trim off strength while keeping a close eye on AI implementation and tariff fallout. A new acquisition would do well to wait for the compression of the multiple or sturdier proof of monetization of the AI bet.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Mining Companies Lead The Calendar As More Sizable IPOs Submit Initial Filings
data.description:
Six issuers debuted in the US this past week, led by two mining companies. Three blank check companies also went public, and a handful of notable IPO candidates joined the pipeline.

Latin American miner Aura Minerals (AUGO) plans to raise $210 million at a $2.1 billion market cap. The company operates four wholly-owned operating mines in various countries in Latin America, as well as one mine in ramp-up phase. It primarily generates revenue from gold and, to a lesser extent, copper, with operations roughly evenly split between Brazil, Mexico, and Honduras. Aura Minerals finished down 1%.

Canada-listed Almonty Industries (ALM) plans to raise $75 million at a roughly $1.5 billion market cap. The company produces tungsten concentrate, primarily for the defense industry, with plans to vertically integrate into oxide production. Its flagship asset, the Sangdong Tungsten Mine in South Korea, is one of the world's largest tungsten deposits by Inferred Mineral Resource and is in soft commissioning. Almonty finished up 1%.

The week’s remaining deals came from small Asian issuers, led by Chinese supply chain management firm Majestic Ideal Holdings (MJID), which raised $15 million and finished down 45%.

9 IPOs During the Week of July 14th, 2025

The week’s filings were led by three that plan to raise $100+ million: digital asset platform Bullish (BLSH), financial exchange operator Miami International (MIAX), and medical imagining firm Heartflow (HTFL). Another notable addition - California-based bank Avidbank (OTC:AVBH) filed for a $70 million uplisting.

Offers imaging and diagnostics software for evaluating coronary artery disease.

Week Ahead

Four sizable IPOs are currently scheduled to list in the week ahead. Some small names may also join the calendar throughout the week.

Consumer intelligence provider NIQ Global Intelligence (NIQ) plans to raise $1.1 billion at a $6.5 billion market cap. The company is a leading global consumer intelligence company, and its global reach spans over 90 countries covering approximately 85% of the world's population, more than half the world's GDP, and more than $7.2 trillion in global consumer spend as of December 31, 2024. It has grown its revenue in recent years, aided by strong retention rates, although it remains loss-making, with a negative free cash flow.

Specialty insurance marketplace Accelerant (ARX) plans to raise $550 million at a $4.2 billion market cap. Accelerant operates a data-driven risk exchange that connects selected specialty insurance underwriters (sellers) with risk capital partners (buyers). On the "supply side" of its Risk Exchange, the company delivers a full service offering to members, while on the "demand side," the company offers risk capital partners a portfolio of specialty insurance premium that may otherwise be difficult to access elsewhere. As of March 31, 2025, Accelerant had 232 members and 96 risk capital partners on its platform and has grown Exchange Written Premium at a 217% CAGR since inception.

Leading educational publisher McGraw Hill (MH) plans to raise $500 million at a $4.0 billion market cap. McGraw Hill is a leading global provider of information solutions for education across K-12 to higher education and through professional learning. On an annual basis, the company serves approximately 60 million learners and educators. McGraw Hill is a top two provider in the K-12 market in the US, serving approximately 99% of public K-12 districts. It is also a top two provider of digital and print learning solutions in the US higher education market, serving approximately 82% of US higher education institutions, although it is highly leveraged at 3.8x net debt/EBITDA.

Spine surgery-focused medtech Carlsmed (CARL) plans to raise $101 million at a $430 million market cap. Its aprevo Technology Platform consists of AI-enabled software solutions, interbody implants custom designed for each patient's unique pathology and vertebral bone topography, and single-use surgical instruments. The company currently markets the aprevo Technology Platform for lumbar spine fusion surgery, and it is further developing the Platform for use in cervical spine fusion surgeries, which it expects to commercialize in 2026. As of March 31, 2025, 177 surgeon users had completed one or more procedures using the aprevo Technology Platform (+72% y/y).

Renaissance Capital's IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Arm (ARM) and Reddit (RDDT). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 24.7% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 18.3%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Galderma and Horizon Robotics.

data.title: Omada Health: Long Growth Runway Ahead
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for Omada Health (NASDAQ:OMDA) is a buy rating. OMDA stands out because it has clinical validation and strong distribution partnerships. These should enable it to capture demand in this massive TAM, and given that it currently only captures a small portion of the market, the growth runway is wide open.

Business Overview

OMDA is a virtual healthcare platform that helps people manage chronic conditions like diabetes, hypertension, and MSK issues. Using human-led clinical coaching with connected devices, OMDA drives results between doctor visits. Adoption has been strong, as OMDA has already enrolled more than one million members across >2,000 employers and health plans.

A Massive, Untapped Market Opportunity

OMDA operates in a very massive market that makes its growth story very attractive. Chronic disease management is a critical thing, and it is a space that accounts for ~90% of annual U.S. healthcare spending, per the S-1 filing. To give a better sense of the size, the total addressable market [TAM] across all OMDA’s core programs (diabetes, prediabetes, hypertension, and MSK) is more than $135 billion. Note that this TAM estimate is not something that requires a leap of faith to believe; these are based on the current percentage of Americans that are already living with these conditions. Which means the TAM could get even larger if we take into account the future aging population, population growth, etc. Given that OMDA only has a revenue size of ~$170 million in FY24, I think it is safe to say there is plenty of room to grow.

Another way to frame the growth runway is to look at OMDA’s capture rate of the US population that is covered by commercial health insurance. There are about 154 million covered people, but OMDA currently only has access to a little over 20 million. The math implies that OMDA has less than 15% penetration, which gives it plenty of grounds to grow.

Clinical Validation Is the Real Moat

I think what really sets OMDA apart from the typical digital health platform provider is that it is backed by actual clinical outcomes. This is not a case of a tech company adding health features; I see it as a healthcare company that has a strong tech background. OMDA’s platform is backed by 29 peer-reviewed publications, and this is a true moat because it adds a lot of credibility to what OMDA is offering, and it is especially useful when it comes to selling to large employers and health plans that are typically pragmatic. To be blunt, these are sophisticated buyers that, I believe, don’t really care about optics or whether it has the most clicks (during the pitching phase). They just want to know if it can generate a good return on investments [ROI], and OMDA’s ability to show proven outcomes is exactly what I expect to help it win deals.

Take diabetes, for instance; OMDA has demonstrated a 2-point A1C reduction in high-risk members. For weight loss, OMDA’s GLP-1 program has shown dramatic weight loss over the first 16 weeks. These are real tangible numbers that clients can use to figure out the ROI they can achieve and also how much claim cost they can save. The broader implication is that as the market matures, players in this industry without any proof to show that their solutions deliver results will not be able to gain any significant traction. To stay competitive, having clinical validation is the only moat, and OMDA already has it.

Strategic Partnerships Unlock Growth

OMDA's go-to-market [GTM] strategy also de-risks the growth path ahead. Instead of relying on its own sales team, which can be effective but is costly and slow, I prefer OMDA’s strategy of relying on its partners, which gives it instant distribution at scale. The best example so far is OMDA’s partnership with Cigna’s Express Scripts to launch EncircleRx. Under this model, members must enroll in Omada’s program to access GLP-1 drugs. This instantly creates built-in demand, allowing OMDA to scale rapidly, and the results speak for themselves: by 1Q25, the program had already reached 9 million covered lives. Although OMDA has to give up some profits, I think it is more important to scale up because with more scale and reach, OMDA is able to innovate faster and push out more products to further enhance its moat.

In addition to scale and reach, this GTM strategy also reduces customer acquisition cost [CAC] and improves revenue visibility. Since the partnership typically means OMDA is embedded into infrastructure like pharmacy benefit managers [PBMs], it turns OMDA into a must-have vendor and not an option. The momentum in ramping up distribution capacity isn’t stopping either. OMDA is now rolling out a similar deal with CVS Caremark. If execution holds up, this CVS deal could be a near-term catalyst that drives growth acceleration and pulls forward OMDA’s timeline to be profitable.

Valuation

Management’s long-term target is for revenue to compound at >20%, which I think is possible given the value proposition and GTM strategy. To model this out, I assumed revenue growth would decline steadily to 20% over the next three years. This may be too conservative, though, since 1Q25 grew revenue by >50%. Nonetheless, this still ties in with the guide of >20% revenue CAGR. OMDA now trades at ~5.7x forward revenue, but since I am expecting revenue growth to slow, I think it's fair to assume that multiples will come down, but some offsetting factor would be that OMDA should turn profitable by FY27. To reflect this, I assumed a ~30% haircut from the current multiple and modeled in 4x forward revenue (readers can opt for their own multiple assumption, as 30% is my own assumption to be conservative).

Risk

A key downside risk is OMDA’s significant customer concentration. In FY24, sales from its top five health plan and PBM partners represented 69% of total revenue. This risk is even worse than it seems when we consider that a single partner, The Cigna Group, accounted for 55% of revenue in FY24. Any loss of these key partners could materially impact OMDA’s financials and growth visibility.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for OMDA. The growth story is very attractive, as the TAM is large and OMDA only captures a small portion of it today. Its moat stems from real clinical outcomes and strong partnerships. While customer concentration is a real risk, so long as OMDA manages the relationship well, it should have no issue achieving management’s guidance of 20%+ revenue growth.

data.title: OPEC Update July 2025
data.description:
A guest post by D C

The OPEC Monthly Oil Market Report (MOMR) for July 2025 was published recently. The last month reported in most of the OPEC charts that follow is June 2025, and output reported for OPEC nations is crude oil output in thousands of barrels per day (kb/d). In the OPEC charts below, the blue line with markers is monthly output, and the thin red line is the centered twelve-month average (CTMA) output.

OPEC 12 output for May 2025 was revised lower by 6 kb/d and April 2025 output was revised lower by 2 kb/d compared to last month’s report. OPEC 12 output increased by 220 kb/d, with the largest increases from Saudi Arabia (173 kb/d), UAE (83 kb/d), Nigeria (19 kb/d), Kuwait (12 kb/d), and Iraq (11 kb/d). Iran (62 kb/d) and Libya (24 kb/d) saw decreased crude output; all other OPEC members had small increases or decreases of 7 kb/d or less.

The chart above shows output from the Big 4 OPEC producers that are subject to output quotas (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iraq, and Kuwait.) After the pandemic, Big 4 average output peaked in 2022 at a centered 12-month average (CTMA) of 20849 kb/d. Crude output has been cut by 2061 kb/d relative to the 2022 CTMA peak to 18788 kb/d in June 2025. The Big 4 may have about 2061 kb/d of spare capacity when World demand calls for an increase in output. Note that peak capacity might be as much as 2200 kb/d higher for a single month, but the important number is output that can be sustained for at least 12 months. My estimate is based on output that has been proven over 12 months rather than capacity that is claimed.

OPEC has announced plans to increase output at about 411 kb/d each month from May to July 2025 and an August increase of 548 kb/d, which would increase output by about 1781 kb/d. The OPEC Big 4 output is expected to increase by 929 kb/d over the next two months. Spare capacity will fall to 1132 kb/d, based on my estimate of OPEC spare capacity. It remains to be seen if the OPEC Big 4 can return to their previous centered 12-month peak attained in November 2022. Iraq may struggle, and there may be only 500 kb/d of spare capacity beyond the August 2025 target output (19717 kb/d for Big 4).

The chart above shows the average annual increase of 143 kb/d for the Other 6 OPEC groups, which excludes the Big 4, Iran and Venezuela. Iran and Venezuela have seen their output rise over the past 3 years at an annual rate of 432 kb/d, which I believe will fall to zero in the near future. The chart below shows the long-term trend for the OPEC Other 6 which is roughly flat (a small average annual rate of decrease of 12 kb/d over the past 11 years.) I expect the Other 6 will see their output return to this flat trend in the next few years.

The World liquids demand forecast is unchanged from last month. Non-DOC liquids and DOC NGLs output has been revised higher this month compared to last month by 200 kb/d in 2024, 300 kb/d in 2025, and 200 kb/d in 2026.

Refinery crude throughput is a better way to determine World demand for C+C (though imperfect because some crude is burned directly in power plants in the middle east.) OPEC data shows the peak was 81.68 Mb/d in 2018. For the most recent 4 quarters, the average refinery throughput was 81.05 Mb/d. The previous 4 quarters had average refinery throughput at 81.08 kb/d, and 2024 had refinery throughput at 81.01 kb/d, so only about a 40 kb/d increase in the annual average over 6 months, which suggests another 8 years to return to the 2018 peak if the recent rate of increase (over the past 6 months) in refinery throughput continues for the next 8 years.

The OPEC forecast for annual change in US tight oil output has been revised from last month’s MOMR with 2024 lower by 2 kb/d, but the forecast change in US tight oil output in 2025 and 2026 remains the same as last month’s forecast.

All of the tight oil increase in 2025 comes from the Permian Basin, and in 2026, Permian Basin output increases by half as much as 2025, but other tight oil plays see decline equal to the Permian increase, so that overall, there is no increase in tight oil output in 2026.

The increases in OPEC output as well as continued increases from Argentina, Brazil, Canada, and Guyana (recent annual increases at about 500 kb/d for all 4 of these nations) lead to a new peak in 2028 at 84.6 Mb/d in the scenario presented above.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Clipper Realty: Upside Potential, But Far From Risk-Free
data.description:
Introduction

After selling around 84% of my EPR Properties (EPR) stock that served me well in the years following the pandemic, it was time to buy into new positions. Even though I funneled a lot towards expanding my position in Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) it was Clipper Realty (NYSE:CLPR) that drew my attention. So I bought around 1000 shares, because I felt that the current share price was way too low on the surface.

Today I will provide my readers with a deep-dive article that will show you the good, the bad, and perhaps even the ugly. In order to assess whether Clipper Realty makes sense to add to my/your portfolio.

(Detailed) Portfolio composition analysis

As a self-managed REIT operation solely in the New York metropolitan area, CLIPR focuses on operating and repositioning multifamily residential and commercial properties. Just by looking at the portfolio map, you can see that this is a very small REIT. With equity trading at around $163 million and a volatile share price (52 week share price range between $3.43 - $7.12) it's fair to say that we are not talking about safe-haven REITs like the famous Realty Income (O) or Agree Realty Income (ADC).

When you take a look at the recent 10-Q, you can see what is in Clipper Realty's portfolio. As you can see, the bulk of its assets are

Dean Street Property

The Dean Street property (last in the list above) is done, so leasing will commence June 1st. Great news, since - especially with small REITs like this one - this leasing-up will contribute positively to [A]FFO. At the same time, tenant incentives (TI) needed to lease up this new asset is done, saving cash.

I'm not sure why the recent 10-Q mentions 9000 square feet of rental square feet, but the recent conference call transcript mentions 19.000 GLA. Must be a typo? Anyways, when you take a look at the Dean Street property website, you can see that only two apartments are currently not leased yet. As a percentage of the total 240 units, +99% of this new property is fully leased.

Considering the fact that the current Manhattan rent is at an all-time high at the time of writing (average unit costing $5450) it's safe to say that the current new Brooklyn asset - brand new - does not seem that expensive from a relative standpoint.

10 West 65th Street and 141 Livingston

During Q1 2025, Clipper Realty has signed a deal to sell the 10 West 65th asset that was still listed in the 10-Q properties list for obvious reasons. Around $12 million in excess cash will be coming in following this deal, after payments of debt and costs. The sale was already planned as far as I can see, being part of "our 2017 purchase acquisition plan." Because free market rental rates are much higher but this asset is restricted from achieving them, I feel it was a prudent step to sell the asset at a profit.

Regarding 141 Livingston, a 15-story office building with 216.000 of gross leasable area, it's great news that a five-year renewal came through during Q1. During the conference call, analyst B. Horne asked about the terms regarding this lease extension. Management said that there are no tenant incentives involved and that they expect the deal to be finalized in the next couple of weeks. As far as I can see, no news is yet published regarding the terms of leasing.

250 Livingston Street

Last February, The City of New York has notified Clipper Realty that it wants to terminate its lease at 250 Livingston Street, effective August 23, 2025. This will reduce rent by $15.4 million. Because of the $125 million building mortgage, a cash management account for the benefit of the lender is in place now. Basically meaning that all revenue generated by 250 Living Street is going straight to this deposit. Only if the loan agreements are satisfied (meaning that a new tenant - at a decent rental rate - must lease the space) this money will be freed up again. You can look up all the details yourself, by checking page 15 of the latest Q1 2025 report SEC filing.

The Good

When we take a look at the Adjusted Funds From Operations (AFFO) we get a feeling of the cash flow that a REIT is generating. It corrects for recurring capital ($35 thousand) and straight-line rent ($120 thousand) among others. Anyways, we get $8 million in AFFO, or $0.19 per share. With a $0.095 dividend, the pay-out ratio is 50%. But the dividend is not safe, more on that later.

The 36% boost in AFFO is mainly attributable to high rental demand. With 99% of properties occupied and combined with a strong rental growth, it makes sense that AFFO can grow quickly.

When we look ahead, the current trends in the real estate market seem to indicate that rental growth will continue to climb, contributing to AFFO.

Last but not least, when we take a look at how Mr. Market tend to value Clipper Realty, we can see that the normalized P/AFFO multiple ranged from 10.4x - 19.55x. FED rates combined with tariffs that are driving up inflation are negatively impacting REITs. REITs use a lot of leverage to boost returns. As we will see in a moment, Clipper Realty is highly leveraged, so any change in interest rates (forecasts) impacts Clipper Realty immediately.

When we take into consideration that the current $0.19 in Q1 AFFO was during the lower income first quarter, this equals to a (Ceteris Paribus) forward looking P/AFFO multiple of around +/- 5.5x when taking the current $4.20 share price. FastGraphs probably takes the trailing AFFO numbers. I use the forward looking multiple, taking the Q1 2025 $0.19 AFFO. My estimation is conservative if all goes well (since AFFO will grow in this scenario). But if 250 Livingston (more on this in the coming chapter) will not find a new tenant in the near future, the AFFO could be meaningfully lower in the - again - near future.

The "Bad"?

250 Livingston Street is creating a lot of uncertainty right now. If re-leasing this building won't succeed (in the foreseeable future) rental income will be reduced by around $15.4 million per year, or around $3.85 million per quarter. Because a vacant property creates additional costs related to real estate taxes, insurance, and maintenance costs, the reduction in AFFO could be more than $3.85 per quarter. I do not expect this asset to remain vacant, but a sale (at a depressed price) or rent concessions to attract a new tenant, could be in the books. But if we just take the worst scenario and we face an empty building associated with around $4 million in reduced AFFO, we are talking about ($8 million - $4 million) $0.095 per share in AFFO, or a 100% pay-out ratio.

Investing activities related to Dean Street and the Flatbush Gardens decreased from + $22 million (Q1 2024) to around $10 million (Q1 2025).

The Flatbush Gardens investing activities relate to a deal that Clipper Realty made with The City of New York back in 2023. As part of a tax exemption, Clipper Realty committed to a three-year capital improvement plan at the property. This should ultimately benefit AFFO per share. In a year, this redevelopment should end.

Debt schedule

When looking at the debt maturity ladder, we can see that there is a lot to be handled with. 10 West 65th Street in Manhattan is already sold, so this $31 million in (relatively high-cost debt) is gone.

The two-year bridge loan for the Dean Street Property is currently bearing a lower interest rate than reported in the most recent 10-Q. These bridge loans are obviously more expensive. We can expect that in the near future, this expensive debt will get swapped for a meaningfully lower interest rate. Especially since none of Clipper Realty's assets is cross collateralized. Meaning that leverage on a company-level is less important to lenders when they finance a (new) asset like the one on Dean Street.

Rent control

New York City's new mayor, Zohran Mamdani, is framed as a die-hard socialist that wants to freeze rents. First of all, the rent freeze would be for rent-stabilized tenants. Next, experts believe that rent stabilization/freezing could ultimately lead to higher free-market rents, since lower rent coming from one part of the tenants could get offset by increasing rent on the tenants that are not protected by this type of legislation.

We have to see what will ultimately happen with rent control/freezes in New York City. It's hard to speculate on this topic for now. But if New York decides to freeze the "rent-stabilized" part of the market, this will impact Clipper Realty as well. Since 30% of the newly delivered 250 residential units on 953 Dean Street are 'affordable' and not 'free market'. The same applies - for example - to the Flatbush Gardens, where all leases at the property have been rent stabilized since inception. The true impact coming from this political shift is still unknown, but it could lead to slower future AFFO growth.

Verdict

When we talk about leverage, it is not only important to look at the amount of debt, but more so to look at the refinancing risk. In this regard, it is good to see that the current interest rate on the debt that needs refinancing soon, is already pretty high. Most of it is related to bridge-loans and is carrying rates way above where the FED-rate currently is. So a short-term headwinds, that's it.

When you take a look at the large amounts of debt on the ladder, we are talking about maturity dates in 2028 ($360 million) or 2032 ($329 million) for example. That's not a real "risk" in my opinion, since it gives Clipper Realty plenty of time to free up funds to deleverage the company.

In the mean time, rental revenue (and therefore AFFO) is growing rapidly. On the back of this, you are getting a $0.095 quarterly dividend that equals to around a 9% dividend yield (at today's $4.20 price as of 18/07/25).

Shares are trading at depressed levels. If we just take a more reasonable 10x P/AFFO multiple, we are looking at a $7.60 share price. Or + 80% total return, excluding dividends. If rates fall, rents keep climbing, and the 250 Livingston Street problem is resolved, this should boot well for Clipper Realty.

On the other hand, if the re-leasing of 250 Livingston Street doesn't work out as planned and Mamdani's goes aggressive on rent controls, shares could trade lower (or sideways) for the foreseeable future.

I just don't think that bankruptcy risk is a real threat when talking about Clipper Realty. I'm evena quite positive about this part of the story, since a lot of expensive short-term debt should be swapped in the near term. Mitigating the financial impact of "rent freeze" risk. In the near future, cashflow towards (re)developments will dry up, freeing up more money to deleverage of even bump the dividend. Even though I would recommend deleveraging.

I'm currently holding on to my recently bought 1000 shares of Clipper Realty. I bought at $3.67, so I think I'm pretty safe for now. But I'm not here for the juicy dividend per se. If the company decides to reallocate capital towards deleveraging for example, I'm fine with that. I'm here for the depressed valuation based on the P/AFFO multiple. I'm also convinced that the market is overreaction to the mentioned risks. They are real, without a doubt, but just look at the current trading range of this stock... I believe that Mr. Market is overreacting again. But I might be wrong. I will not buy more shares though, since Clipper Realty is running a concentrated and small portfolio.

I will keep an eye out going into Q2 2025. Especially relating to AFFO growth and any updates regarding the portfolio, especially 250 Livingston Street.

Cheers!

data.title: Global Water Resources: A Growth Company Disguised As A Utility
data.description:
Global Water Resources (NASDAQ:GWRS) acts as a utility and has a P/E of 55x. Despite this, it is the only TWM pure-play in the state of Arizona, with an upside potential of 33% caused by the expected intensification of the water crisis.

Company Summary:

Global Water Resources is a company in the water management utilities sector founded in 2003 and currently serves about 111,000 Americans in 36,000 homes. The company operates in Arizona, one of the major centers of population migration that, due to its geographical characteristics, is more prone to facing water crises, as it is currently experiencing.

A high-interest rate environment, with an increase in expenses and reductions in non-recurring revenues like ICFAs, has been pressuring the stock value in recent years. It has been in a stagnation zone for the last 5 years, and some of the stock's characteristics are:

  • Beta: 1.04x
  • Dividend Yield: 2.91% (FWD)
  • P/E: 55x (FWD)
  • EV/EBITDA: 16.5x (LTM)
  • ROE: 9.11%

In 1Q25 its revenue was segregated between two main segments, which are:

  • Sewage and Recycled Water Services: representing $6.48 million (52% of revenue) and;
  • Drinking Water Services: $5.98 million (48% of revenue).

The company operates on a business model called "Total Water Management" (TWM) which involves sewage treatment and water supply in all its phases, which characterizes a certain premium for the company, especially in regions with water scarcity and with regulatory barriers where a water supplier must prove project sustainability for the next 100 years to be approved in Arizona.

In my opinion, one of the most important factors that must be analyzed is: 55x Forward P/E, and a beta higher than 1. This company certainly does not trade as if it were in the utilities sector, and it has been this way for more than 3 years. Why?

In general, the value of a utility is tied to predictability and stability, predictable cash flows, and low volatility. This is what usually attributes low P/E and a low beta to companies in the sector. For GWRS, like the market average, it is priced by future expectations. This is because unlike other older utilities, this one founded in 2003 was started on the premise of Total Water Management. While a large part of the sector uses legacy versions and has limited growth, GWRS provides a new technology, being the best equipped to deal with the region's water problems. In short, the market understands that it is the right Model (TWM) in the right place (Arizona) at the right time (projected population growth along with the water crisis).

Sector:

As previously stated, Arizona is one of the states with the highest population growth, ranking among the 6th with the highest population influx. In the last 3 years, the average population growth per year was 1.7%, a substantial growth. It is projected to surpass 9 million by 2040 (an 18% growth compared to the current value of 7.58 Million). Given that the Arizona government only allows investments in water if they prove 100 years of sustainability in the water supply, the company positions itself as one of the preferred within the region.

Although other utilities that operate in Arizona are also in the process of adapting to TWM, such as (EPCOR), GWRS presents two differences, the first being that this business model has been implemented in it since its beginning, with no adaptations or reconstructions being necessary. The second is that it is exclusively focused on the Arizona market, so all available growth is the growth expected in Arizona, while its diversified Peers also have more moderate growth expectations from other states.

It is also expected that water consumption will surpass supply by 40% by 2030, driven mostly by energy use and population growth. Although GWRS does not sell water directly to the clients, and instead are remunerated through regulated tariffs, an (inevitable) increase of water consumption would indirectly cause an increase in the rate case permitted by the authorities, moved by the need for new infrastructure, what would increase the company's Rate Base and consequently would permit even bigger revenues, forming a virtuous cycle that should last until the regularization of the water crisis.

Besides, decades of drought in the Colorado River caused by over use and climate changes, made its main reservoirs, like Lake Mead and Lake Powell, arrive at extremely low levels. As a result, Arizona faced cuts in the receiving of water through the Central Arizona Project (CAP), this drastic reduction in the supply occasioned an increase in the dependence on subterranean water.

Subterranean waters are already being overexploited in several basins, with a projection of a deficit of 4.86 million acre-feet in Phoenix in the next 100 years, what should make it each time more economically unviable to pump water from the basins.. What in my opinion are two big indicators that the water crisis will take longer to stabilize in Arizona than in other regions, driving the company's growth. And GWRS is the only publicly traded company that provides a pure exposure to TWM inside of Arizona, explaining the historic high premium.

Financials & Valuation:

The financial perspectives for the company remain positive, although it presents an apparent stagnation at first sight, the decrease in revenue from 2023 to 2024 was due to a non-recurring project (ICFA) that occasioned a revenue of US$ 2.8 Million in 2023. While the “Core” revenue was US$ 50.2 million. In 2024, the core revenue grew to US$ 52.7 million, a growth in the main business of 5%, while it kept the operational margins stable, comparatively to previous years.

Looking at the Earnings, the company also remains stable, although the EPS is not growing substantially, the relative stability between 2022 and 2024 is explained by an additional interest expense and a dilution of the shares. The High Payout although not sustainable, is also not scary, given that the depreciation (US$ 12.7 million in 2024) is not a cash expense, and given the revenue stability of the utilities sector I do not think that the Payout over 100% is as worrisome as it seems since despite having a Net Income of only US$ 5.8 Million in 2024, it had an operational cash flow of US$ 20.1 million in the same period, covering the amount paid in dividends with ease.

The company's EV/EBITDA multiple is also far from the average it usually trades at. Although I do not believe that levels of 30x will be presented again, I also imagine that a return to levels of 18x is plausible especially when the Rate Cuts materialize, making the dividends that the company offers to income investors more pleasant.

My valuation started from the premise of an EBITDA growth of 9% from the 2024 figure, a slight decrease in debt, and an EV/EBITDA Multiple stabilized around 16.5, arriving at a target price of US$ 11.13 per share in 12 months. An upside of 13% from today's value of US$ 10.21.

After the rate cuts and with the intensification of the water scarcity narrative, I project a multiple of 18x. Along with a 1% increase in the company's EBITDA Margin and a revenue growth at the same projected level of 2026. I also expect a dilution in the shares and a higher level of debt, implying a target price of US$ 13.61 in 18-24 months. An upside of 33% from the current price, excluding the dividends of 3% per year in the period. Being at the Lower End of the Wall St. Target, which has Average Price Target of US$ 15.5.

The downside potential is also low. The stock presents a solid support line around US$ 9.3 (-9% from the current value) and also as said before is near the historical lows of the EV/EBITDA value, along with a dividend yield of 3%, these three factors in my opinion represent an extremely strong downside protection, with losses limited to 9%, and that in my opinion would recover.

Drivers & Risks

Short term

The company has a pending tariff adjustment case with the ACC, seeking a revenue increase of US$ 6.5 million in Pinal County. In case there is an approval (even if partial) it would increase the EBITDA and the margins substantially, immediately.

An increase or the extension of the interest rates in the United States represents a substantial risk for the company. Besides a large part of the value being unlocked when the interest rates fall, the high rates increase the interest expenses and limit the company's growth.

Long term

The water crisis is a factor expected globally to intensify starting from 2030. Arizona is a desert with a population growth of 1.5% per year that is overloading the use of subterranean waters and has limitations in the use of the rivers' waters. The company with the TWM is the best pure play positioned based on these characteristics, what substantiates the narrative behind its multiples, paired with the sector.

These factors in my opinion position the company for an eminent intensification maof the water crisis, representing its biggest Driver.

However, in case suddenly Arizona stops having population growth, and consequently offered fewer connected homes, or the limitations of the reserves of the Colorado River's water decrease the upside may take longer to materialize. Representing its biggest risk.

Conclusion

In my view, the company is literally in the right place and at the right time, also presenting the ideal model (TWM). I believe that the company's high P/E (55x) masks the future perspectives for it.

Being near historical lows and presenting a dividend yield of 3%, it demonstrates a great downside cushion while it presents a potential upside of 33% in 2 years. With this, my recommendation is a STRONG BUY, with a target price of US$ 13.61.
